%%
%% The abstract is a short summary of the work to be presented in the
%% article.
\begin{abstract}
\hspace*{\parindent}Architectural drift occurs when an implementation gradually diverges from its intended design constraints. Prior work has shown that LLMs and static analysis can help recover or detect architectural structure, but recovery alone does not prevent future violations. This project evaluates a hybrid pipeline that translates architecture documentation into executable enforcement artifacts: ArchUnit suites for Java repositories and arch-go configurations for Go repositories. The pipeline uses LLMs to generate and review candidate rules, while deterministic build and enforcement tools provide the final pass/fail judgment.

We evaluate the approach on Spring Framework,\linebreak Apache ZooKeeper, and HashiCorp Consul using two models, \linebreak \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} and \texttt{gemini-3-flash} for the generation phase, paired with a reviewer using \texttt{claude-opus-4-7}. All six model-repository combinations eventually reached executable suites with 0 baseline violations, but only after compile fixes, schema fixes, mapping corrections, and reviewer-guided triage. \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} required fewer total iterations and produced stronger ZooKeeper and Consul suites, while \texttt{gemini-3-flash} produced the stronger Spring Framework suite. Pull request evaluation showed suite-dependent drift detection, including a ZooKeeper pull request that failed under one generated suite but passed under the other, and \due{Consul pull request drift result}. The results suggest that LLMs can generate useful architectural enforcement rules, but reliable drift prevention requires deterministic tooling and repository-aware engineer oversight.
\end{abstract}

\section{Introduction}
Throughout the continuous lifecycle of software maintenance and evolution, systems inevitably suffer from architectural drift as the implemented codebase gradually diverges from its prescriptive design constraints. This degradation is introduced by both human developers through ad hoc modifications and by AI coding agents that generate locally correct but structurally harmful code due to their reliance on localized context windows. While recent advances have enabled automated architecture recovery from source code with high accuracy, these approaches remain reactive. They reconstruct architecture after drift has already occurred but provide no mechanism to prevent it from recurring. This project aims to close that gap by automatically translating recovered architectures into enforceable build-time constraints that block violations before they are merged into production.

\subsection{Motivation}
Addressing architectural drift proactively is critical because unchecked structural degradation quietly accumulates massive technical debt that ultimately paralyzes a system's capacity for long-term maintenance and evolution. This challenge grows more urgent as development teams increasingly adopt autonomous AI coding agents that lack awareness of system-level design constraints. Software architects and maintenance engineers will directly benefit from this research by gaining an automated enforcement pipeline that continuously validates proposed changes against the intended architecture. Consequently, development teams can integrate both human contributors and AI agents into their workflows while preserving the structural integrity and sustainability of their complex systems.

\section{Related work}
Recent literature has increasingly explored the intersection of LLMs and software architecture, but efforts to use AI for detecting architectural drift have revealed significant limitations.
\subsection{Exploring Architectural Smells Detection Through LLMs}
Tessa et al.~\cite{tessa2025} investigated the use of off-the-shelf LLMs to detect structural symptoms of drift, such as architectural smells. Specifically, they evaluated Google's Gemini 1.5 Pro on its ability to detect Hub-like Dependencies (HL) in open-source Java projects, comparing its performance against Arcan, a specialized architectural smell detection tool. An HL is a critical smell where a component has excessive incoming and outgoing dependencies.
The study revealed a severe precision and explainability gap. While the LLM achieved 100\% recall in spotting high-dependency components, its precision varied significantly. More detailed prompts improved detection performance from 64\% to 82\% for lower-severity smells, yet the model still could not reliably distinguish architecturally significant dependencies from benign ones. Furthermore, only 49\% of the LLM's explanations for the detected smells were deemed satisfactory by human evaluators. This indicates that the model relies on superficial pattern matching rather than genuine architectural reasoning.

\subsection{Towards Automated Identification of Violation Symptoms of Architecture Erosion}
Li et al.~\cite{li2023} explored the automated identification of architecture erosion by analyzing developer discussions in code reviews. They developed 15 machine learning-based and 4 deep learning-based classifiers using three pre-trained word embeddings to detect violation symptoms from code review comments across four large open-source projects: OpenStack Nova, OpenStack Neutron, Qt Base, and Qt Creator. They further compared these traditional classifiers against LLMs such as GPT-4o, Qwen-2.5, and DeepSeek-R1. The best performing traditional classifier was SVM with word2vec, achieving an F1-score of 0.779.
This approach is fundamentally reactive and dependent on human oversight. It only succeeds when a developer has already noticed the violation and discussed it in a code review comment. Because the classifiers evaluate natural language discussions rather than the underlying structural graph of the codebase, they remain entirely blind to silent drift, which refers to subtle architectural violations that bypass human reviewers during routine feature updates and are never documented in any textual artifact.

\subsection{ArchAgent: Scalable Legacy Software Architecture Recovery with LLMs}
In one of the most advanced attempts to handle large-scale systems, Pan et al.~\cite{pan2026} introduced ArchAgent, a hybrid framework designed to recover architectural design from legacy software. It combines traditional static analysis using Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs) with LLM-powered semantic synthesis to reconstruct architecture diagrams from cross-repository codebases. In their evaluation across eight large-scale GitHub projects, ArchAgent achieved a mean F1 score of 0.966, demonstrating that accurate architecture recovery from source code is feasible.

However, the authors explicitly highlight the local-scope bias and context limitations of modern LLMs as a primary hurdle. Industrial codebases frequently exceed millions of tokens, which overwhelms the context windows of even the most advanced models. More critically, ArchAgent treats recovery as a one-time diagnostic event. Once the architecture is reconstructed, no mechanism exists to enforce it against future changes, leaving the recovered architecture vulnerable to the same drift that necessitated its reconstruction.

\subsection{Formal Software Architecture Rule Learning: A Comparative Investigation between Large Language Models and Inductive Techniques}
Schindler and Rausch \cite{schindler2024} investigate the use of machine learning techniques to automatically infer software architecture rules from implementation data, addressing the limitations of manual rule specification, which is often time-consuming and error-prone. The study compares inductive rule learning (IRL) approaches with LLMs by applying both to a knowledge base derived from real-world software systems. Their results show that IRL methods, particularly tools like Popper, are generally more effective and consistent in learning rules that align with predefined architectural hypotheses and demonstrate reasonable generalizability across system subsets. In contrast, LLMs exhibit limitations, struggling with formal rule induction, especially when semantic cues are reduced or negative examples are introduced, though they can infer simple rules under favorable conditions. Overall, the paper highlights both the promise and current constraints of data-driven approaches for architectural rule discovery, suggesting that while ML techniques can support more adaptive software architecture practices, further research is needed to improve their robustness and scalability.

\subsection{Evaluating Large Language Models for Detecting Architectural Decision Violations}
Su et al.~\cite{su2026evaluating} evaluate LLMs for detecting architectural decision violations from Architectural Decision Records across 109 GitHub repositories and 980 ADRs. Their results show substantial agreement between models and strong accuracy for explicit decisions that are inferable from code. Accuracy degrades for implicit decisions and deployment-oriented decisions that depend on configuration and organizational context. These findings support using LLMs for translation and review while relying on deterministic enforcement to judge architectural compliance.

Collectively, these studies highlight two compounding limitations. First, LLMs are not reliable as direct judges of architectural compliance. They operate with local context and often lack the system-level and operational information needed to interpret implicit constraints, which leads to false positives and false negatives in violation detection \cite{tessa2025,su2026evaluating}. Second, even when architecture recovery succeeds with high accuracy, recovery alone does not prevent future drift \cite{pan2026}. This project addresses both gaps by treating documentation as ground truth and using LLMs as translators and reviewers. The output is executed as deterministic enforcement rules that continuously validate code changes. This moves beyond rule inference alone \cite{schindler2024} by integrating translated constraints into an enforcement pipeline that can run on baselines and pull requests.


\section{Proposed solution \& tool}
\subsection{Solution}
We propose an automated enforcement pipeline that translates recovered software architectures into executable build-time constraints, see Figure~\ref{fig:solution}. The pipeline takes an architecture representation and a project package structure. It prompts an LLM to generate enforceable rules in the native format of deterministic tools. For Java repositories, it generates ArchUnit test suites. For Go repositories, it generates arch-go configurations. This extends the rule-learning approach of Schindler and Rausch \cite{schindler2024} by operationalizing learned constraints as continuously executable checks. The goal is to prevent architectural drift before it is merged into production rather than recovering architecture reactively. The LLM acts as a translator. ArchUnit and arch-go act as deterministic judges, which avoids relying on LLM reasoning as the final compliance decision \cite{su2026evaluating}.

\begin{figure*}[t]
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{cs6356 solution.png}
    \caption{Workflow Diagram.}
    \label{fig:solution}
\end{figure*}

\subsubsection{Input}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Target software repositories
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Java: Spring Framework and Apache ZooKeeper
        \item Go: HashiCorp Consul
    \end{itemize}
    \item Ground truth architecture documentation provided by the ArchAgent benchmark for each target repository
    \item Extracted package directory structures and package structure listings from the source tree
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Output}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Executable enforcement artifacts that encode the intended architecture as deterministic dependency constraints
    \item Java artifacts
    \begin{itemize}
        \item ArchUnit test suite
        \item Standalone Maven test harness
    \end{itemize}
    \item Go artifacts
    \begin{itemize}
        \item arch-go YAML rule file
        \item Standalone Makefile that runs \texttt{arch-go check}
    \end{itemize}
    \item Supporting reports for review and analysis that record compilation fixes, adversarial review findings, and violation triage results
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Algorithm and Data Transformations}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Input collection: Collect the ground truth architecture documentation and the extracted package structure for the target repository.
    \item Rule generation: Provide the documentation and package structure to an LLM and generate enforcement artifacts. Java outputs are ArchUnit test suites and a standalone Maven test harness. Go outputs are arch-go YAML rules and a standalone Makefile.
    \item Compilation fix loop: Execute the generated artifacts. If the build fails, provide the compilation errors to the model and regenerate the artifacts. Record each correction in the fix history.
    \item Adversarial review and patching: Send the generated rules to a reviewer model for adversarial review against the same documentation. Use \texttt{claude-opus-4-7} as the reviewer model. Apply the recommended patches and record the changes in the fix history.
    \item Baseline validation and triage loop: Run the rules against the repository baseline and collect violations. Use the violation analysis prompt to triage each violation as a mapping error or a real violation using the ground truth documentation. Patch mapping errors and rerun until mapping errors reach zero.
    \item Calibration: Use the final thoughts prompt to write a final alignment pass. State what the passing rules prove. State what remains uncertain due to documentation gaps.
    \item Pull request evaluation: Fetch open pull requests and run the same enforcement artifacts on each pull request branch. Compare results against the baseline. Report new violations introduced by each pull request.
    \item Model comparison: Repeat the pipeline using \texttt{gemini-3-flash} and \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} for generation. Use \texttt{claude-opus-4-7} for review. Compare the resulting enforcement suites and fix histories.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection{SME Data Incorporated}
The solution incorporates ground truth architecture documentation from the ArchAgent benchmark, package-level structural data extracted from each repository, and pull request branches used to evaluate drift introduced by proposed changes.

\subsection{Justification}
The technical motivation for this solution stems from a critical gap in the current architecture recovery landscape. As highlighted by Pan et al.~\cite{pan2026}, LLMs suffer from local scope bias and cannot hold an entire system dependency matrix in their context window. ArchAgent demonstrates that accurate recovery is achievable despite these limitations. Recovered architectures still lack an enforcement mechanism. Without continuous validation, the same development pressures that caused drift will degrade the recovered architecture again.

This solution makes sense because it shifts architectural governance from a reactive diagnosis to a proactive safeguard. Prior work shows that LLM accuracy is highest when constraints are explicit and inferable, and drops when decisions depend on missing context \cite{su2026evaluating}. This pipeline uses an LLM to translate documentation into executable constraints. Deterministic tools then evaluate the code. ArchUnit serves as the judge for Java repositories. arch-go serves as the judge for Go repositories. This separation reduces ambiguity and removes hallucination risk from the pass or fail decision.

The pipeline also includes cross model validation. A reviewer model audits the generated rules against the same documentation. This adversarial review targets missing constraints, incorrect mappings, and rules that would pass vacuously. The review results are applied as patches and recorded in the fix history.

Model comparison strengthens the justification. Rule generation is evaluated using \texttt{gemini-3-flash} and \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}. \texttt{claude-opus-4-7} is used as the reviewer model. This shows whether smaller generation models can still produce enforceable constraints when paired with deterministic enforcement and an adversarial review step.

\subsection{Design and Implementation}
\subsubsection{Type of Tool}
\begin{itemize}
    \item IDE based workflow driven by prompt templates
    \item Command line enforcement checks that run during validation
    \item Repository scoped enforcement artifacts that can be run locally or in CI
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Technologies}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Development environment
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Cursor IDE
        \item Antigravity IDE
    \end{itemize}
    \item Enforcement libraries
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Java: ArchUnit
        \item Go: arch-go
    \end{itemize}
    \item Execution harnesses
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Java: standalone Maven harness
        \item Go: standalone Makefile harness that runs \texttt{arch-go check}
    \end{itemize}
    \item Source control
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Git
    \end{itemize}
    \item Models
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Generation models: \texttt{gemini-3-flash} and \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}
        \item Reviewer model: \texttt{claude-opus-4-7}
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Architecture}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Inputs
    \begin{itemize}
        \item ArchAgent ground truth architecture documentation
        \item Extracted package structure for the target repository
    \end{itemize}
    \item Generation and correction
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Generate Java ArchUnit tests or Go arch-go rules
        \item Fix compilation failures and record changes in the fix history
        \item Review rules against the same documentation and apply patches
    \end{itemize}
    \item Validation and evaluation
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Run against a baseline and triage violations until mapping errors reach zero
        \item Produce a final thoughts calibration report
        \item Run on pull request branches and report new drift
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Interaction Mechanism}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item The ArchAgent documentation and extracted package structure are provided as prompt inputs
    \item The generation prompt is executed to produce enforcement artifacts
    \item The compilation fix prompt is applied when builds fail
    \item The review prompt is executed and the resulting patches are integrated
    \item The violation analysis prompt is iterated until mapping errors reach zero
    \item The final thoughts prompt is executed for output calibration
    \item The artifacts are deployed on pull request branches and validated against the baseline
\end{enumerate}

\section{Empirical evaluation}
\subsection{Evaluation setup}
\subsubsection{Evaluation Goal}
To demonstrate that ground truth architecture documentation can be translated into enforceable build time constraints. The constraints are executed as deterministic checks for Java and Go repositories. The evaluation measures whether the checks detect drift in pull requests before merge.

\subsubsection{Research Questions}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item RQ1: Can LLMs translate ground truth architecture documentation into correct and executable enforcement artifacts for Java and Go repositories?
    \item RQ2: Do the generated enforcement artifacts detect new architectural violations in open pull requests when compared against a baseline run on the main branch?
    \item RQ3: For rule generation, how do \texttt{gemini-3-flash} and \newline\texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} compare in correctness and effort when paired with a \texttt{claude-opus-4-7} reviewer model?
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection{Success Criteria}
    \begin{enumerate}
    \item RQ1 will be considered positively answered if the generated artifacts execute successfully. The Java artifacts must compile and run under the Maven harness. The Go artifacts must run under the Makefile harness and arch-go. Mapping errors must be reduced to zero in the triage loop.
    \item RQ2 will be considered positively answered if the artifacts detect at least one new violation in an open pull request that is not present in the baseline. If no open pull requests trigger violations, the pipeline must detect a deliberately introduced violation in a controlled fork.
    \item RQ3 will be considered positively answered if \texttt{gemini-3-flash} and \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} both reach a stable enforcement suite. The comparison uses the same reviewer model and the same target repositories. The outcome is reported using the resulting artifacts and fix histories.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection{Datasets}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Java repositories
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Spring Framework
        \item Apache ZooKeeper
    \end{itemize}
    \item Go repositories
    \begin{itemize}
        \item HashiCorp Consul
    \end{itemize}
    \item Ground truth architecture documentation from the ArchAgent benchmark for each repository
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Data statistics}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Repositories: Three repositories were evaluated: two Java repositories, Spring Framework and Apache ZooKeeper, and one Go repository, HashiCorp Consul.
    \item Models: Two generation models were evaluated for each repository, \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} and \texttt{gemini-3-flash}, producing six model-repository combinations.
    \item Rule artifacts: The study produced six executable enforcement suites: four Java ArchUnit/Maven suites and two Go arch-go/Make suites.
    \item Pull requests: Six pull requests were evaluated in the completed PR analysis: three Spring Framework pull requests and three Apache ZooKeeper pull requests. HashiCorp Consul PR evaluation is recorded as \due{Consul PR count and identifiers}.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Metrics}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Execution success: Whether Java artifacts compile and run under Maven, and whether Go artifacts run under Make and arch-go.
    \item Mapping error count: The number of violation patterns classified as mapping errors at each triage iteration until the count reaches zero.
    \item Cross model catch rate: The number of missing or incorrect constraints identified by the reviewer model during adversarial review.
    \item Pull request drift detection: The number of new violations in pull request branches that are not present in the baseline.
    \item Effort and convergence: The number of compilation and review fixes recorded in the fix history before reaching a stable suite.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Procedures}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Select a target repository and obtain the ArchAgent ground truth documentation.
    \item Extract the repository package structure used for mapping constraints to packages and import paths.
    \item Generate enforcement artifacts using the generation prompt.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Java repositories produce ArchUnit artifacts executed using the Maven harness.
        \item Go repositories produce arch-go artifacts executed using the Makefile harness and \texttt{arch-go check}.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Resolve build failures using the compilation fix prompt. Record each change in the fix history.
    \item Run adversarial review using \texttt{claude-opus-4-7}. Apply review patches and record changes in the fix history.
    \item Establish a baseline by executing the artifacts on the main branch and recording violations.
    \item Evaluate open pull requests by checking out each pull request branch and executing the same artifacts. Record new violations that are not present in the baseline.
    \item Repeat the procedure for model comparison using \texttt{gemini-3-flash} and \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} as generation models while keeping \texttt{claude-opus-4-7} as the reviewer model.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Evaluation results and analysis}
\subsubsection{Evaluation results}
The results are organized by repository.

\paragraph{Spring Framework.}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Execution: Both generated ArchUnit suites eventually compiled and ran under the Maven harness.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: required one compile fix for a hallucinated ArchUnit method.
        \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: required one compile fix for a missing ArchUnit import.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Baseline: Both suites reached 0 architectural violations and 0 remaining mapping errors.
    \item Review: The reviewer model identified missing or incorrect constraints in both suites.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: 12 initial issues.
        \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: 14 initial issues.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Effort: Table~\ref{tab:convergence-spring} reports compile failures, schema failures, and mapping-error counts by round.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: converged in 6 total iterations.
        \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: converged in 10 total iterations.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Outcome: \texttt{gemini-3-flash} produced the stronger Spring result.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: reached a passing suite with less effort, but used broader suppressions and synthetic layering that weakened alignment with the documented Spring module structure.
        \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: required more iterations, but its eight-layer structure more closely matched the documented Spring module architecture.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Apache ZooKeeper.}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Execution: Both generated ArchUnit suites eventually compiled and ran under the Maven harness.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: compiled initially, but the Review~\#6 patch introduced a Java generics compile error in round 7; it was fixed in round 8.
        \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: repeatedly hallucinated the non-existent ArchUnit method\newline
        \mbox{\texttt{mayBeAccessedByAllLayers()}}, producing four compile-fix iterations.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Baseline: Both suites reached 0 architectural violations and 0 remaining mapping errors.
    \item Review: The reviewer model caught missing or incorrect constraints in both suites.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: 11 initial issues.
        \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: 10 initial issues.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Effort: Table~\ref{tab:convergence-zookeeper} reports compile failures, schema failures, and mapping-error counts by round.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: converged in 8 total iterations.
        \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: converged in 14 total iterations.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Outcome: \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} produced the stronger ZooKeeper artifact.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: introduced a Protocol layer that correctly separates shared wire-format types from the client and server tiers.
        \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: eventually reached a passing suite, but repeatedly hallucinated ArchUnit APIs and relied on flatter package assignments.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{HashiCorp Consul.}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Execution: Both generated arch-go suites eventually ran under the Makefile harness.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: had no compile failure, but review found vacuous arch-go content rules caused by unsupported YAML schema shapes.
        \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: had multiple arch-go API/schema hallucinations, including a non-existent \texttt{maxComplexity} field, a non-existent \texttt{arch-go check --config} invocation, and one YAML unmarshal regression that temporarily disabled rule evaluation.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Baseline: Both suites reached a final baseline with 0 architectural violations.
    \item Review: The reviewer model identified missing or incorrect constraints in both suites.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: 10 initial issues.
        \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: 16 initial issues.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Effort: The Consul fix histories did not tabulate per-round mapping-error counts, so Table~\ref{tab:convergence-consul} reports schema/API correction rounds and the final available value.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: converged in 5 total iterations.
        \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: converged in 3 total iterations.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Outcome: \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} produced the stronger Consul artifact.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: modeled the Server/Consensus tier and intra-server boundaries more precisely.
        \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: converged quickly and added useful harness isolation, but its flatter model collapsed the server into the broader agent layer.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Pull request drift detection.}
Table~\ref{tab:pr-drift} summarizes the pull request drift results by repository.
\begin{itemize}
    \item Spring Framework: Three pull requests were evaluated.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item PR-36701:
        \begin{itemize}
            \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: PASS.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Rationale: The native-mode proxy callback changes stayed within allowed Core/AOP dependency directions.
            \end{itemize}
            \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: PASS.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Rationale: The same changes did not introduce new cross-layer Spring-internal dependencies.
            \end{itemize}
        \end{itemize}
        \item PR-36650:
        \begin{itemize}
            \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: PASS.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Rationale: The messaging change was logic-only and did not alter the dependency graph.
            \end{itemize}
            \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: PASS.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Rationale: The branch stayed within the existing messaging mapping and introduced no new forbidden imports.
            \end{itemize}
        \end{itemize}
        \item PR-36647:
        \begin{itemize}
            \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: PASS.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Rationale: The zstd resource-resolver change stayed within Web and allowed foundational dependencies.
            \end{itemize}
            \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: PASS.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Rationale: The third-party codec did not create a new Spring-internal layer violation.
            \end{itemize}
        \end{itemize}
    \end{itemize}
    \item Apache ZooKeeper: Three pull requests were evaluated.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item PR-2307:
        \begin{itemize}
            \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: \textbf{FAIL}.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Rationale: The module split introduced root-level changes that triggered the suite's stricter Protocol-layer and root-package classification.
            \end{itemize}
            \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: PASS.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Rationale: Its broader root-package handling did not classify the same branch as new drift.
            \end{itemize}
        \end{itemize}
        \item PR-2308:
        \begin{itemize}
            \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: PASS.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Rationale: The added client-operation interfaces did not introduce new forbidden dependency edges.
            \end{itemize}
            \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: PASS.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Rationale: The same changes stayed within its public API/client mapping.
            \end{itemize}
        \end{itemize}
        \item PR-2375:
        \begin{itemize}
            \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: PASS.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Rationale: The export/import CLI changes did not add new disallowed CLI-to-server dependencies.
            \end{itemize}
            \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: PASS.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Rationale: The branch did not introduce new violations against its tools/server rules.
            \end{itemize}
        \end{itemize}
    \end{itemize}
    \item HashiCorp Consul: Pull request evaluation was not completed in this phase and remains a \due{future evaluation item}.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \due{Consul PR identifier}:
        \begin{itemize}
            \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: \due{PASS/FAIL}
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Rationale: \due{Reason for Consul PR result under \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}}
            \end{itemize}
            \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: \due{PASS/FAIL}
            \begin{itemize}
                \item Rationale: \due{Reason for Consul PR result under \texttt{gemini-3-flash}}
            \end{itemize}
        \end{itemize}
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

        \toprule

                                        & 36650 & PASS & PASS \\
                                        & 36647 & PASS & PASS \\

Overall, all six model-repository combinations reached executable stable suites with 0 baseline violations. \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} required less total convergence effort across repositories, 19 iterations versus 27 for \texttt{gemini-3-flash} (Table~\ref{tab:convergence-total}). \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} produced the stronger suites for ZooKeeper and Consul, while \texttt{gemini-3-flash} produced the stronger suite for Spring Framework.

\subsubsection{Discussion}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Interpretation of major findings: The evaluation shows that LLM-generated enforcement artifacts can reach executable, zero-violation baselines for both Java and Go repositories, but the quality of the resulting suite depends strongly on how well the model's architectural assumptions match the target system. This is consistent with prior findings that LLMs are more reliable when architectural constraints are explicit and harder to use when rules depend on implicit system context \cite{schindler2024,su2026evaluating}.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}: performed better on ZooKeeper and Consul, where the architecture required interpreting implicit legacy boundaries such as ZooKeeper's Protocol layer and Consul's Server/Consensus tier.
        \item \texttt{gemini-3-flash}: performed better on Spring Framework, where a stricter and more uniform layered interpretation matched the documented module structure more closely.
        \item The model comparison therefore does not show one universally better generator. It shows that architectural fidelity is system-dependent.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Explanations for failures, mapping errors, and fixes: Most failures fell into three categories. These failures reflect the same limitation identified in prior work: LLMs can help recover or reason about architecture, but they should not be trusted as unvalidated compliance oracles \cite{tessa2025,su2026evaluating}.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Build and API hallucinations: Java suites failed when generated code referenced non-existent ArchUnit APIs or omitted required imports. The clearest case was ZooKeeper with \texttt{gemini-3-flash}, which repeatedly reintroduced\newline{\texttt{mayBeAccessedByAllLayers()}}.
        \item Schema and harness errors: Go suites failed or became partially vacuous when generated YAML used unsupported arch-go schema shapes or Makefile commands. These issues did not always appear as ordinary compilation failures, but they still prevented reliable enforcement.
        \item Mapping errors: Large violation counts usually reflected mismatches between documentation-level concepts and concrete package structure. Fixes converged by reclassifying packages, introducing missing architectural layers, or adding narrow exceptions for known cross-cutting utilities.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Implications for preventing architectural drift: The results support using LLMs as translators and reviewers, but not as final judges. This extends architecture-recovery work by adding executable enforcement after recovery rather than stopping at reconstructed design knowledge \cite{pan2026}.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Deterministic tools remain necessary because they expose compile errors, schema errors, and concrete dependency violations that the generator may miss.
        \item A zero-violation baseline is necessary but not sufficient. It must be interpreted with the fix history, because a suite can pass by using broad suppressions or overly permissive mappings.
        \item Pull request detection is suite-dependent. ZooKeeper PR-2307 failed under \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} but passed under \texttt{gemini-3-flash}, showing that independently generated rule suites are not interchangeable even when they come from the same architecture documentation.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Hybrid Approach: The workflow should therefore be hybrid. LLMs are useful for translating architecture documentation into candidate ArchUnit and arch-go rules, but they should not be treated as final architectural authorities. Deterministic enforcement tools and repository-aware engineer review are needed to validate mappings, inspect suppressions, and distinguish real drift from documentation or modeling errors \cite{schindler2024,su2026evaluating}.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Answers to research questions}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item RQ1: Yes, with correction. Both generation models produced executable ArchUnit or arch-go enforcement artifacts for all three repositories, and all final suites reached 0 baseline violations. However, the path to execution required compile, schema, and mapping fixes. This means LLMs can translate architecture documentation into executable rules, but the rules must be validated through deterministic build and enforcement tools, which aligns with prior evidence that LLM-generated architectural rules need external validation \cite{schindler2024,su2026evaluating}.
    \item RQ2: Yes, but detection was suite-dependent. The evaluated Spring Framework pull requests produced no new violations under either suite. ZooKeeper PR-2307 introduced new violations under the \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} suite but passed under the \texttt{gemini-3-flash} suite. Thus, the evaluation detected at least one new PR-level drift case, but also showed that drift detection depends on the concrete generated rule set. This supports prior work that architectural drift detection is sensitive to the representation of the architectural constraint being checked \cite{tessa2025,li2023}.
    \item RQ3: \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} required less total convergence effort, 19 iterations versus 27 for \texttt{gemini-3-flash}, and produced the stronger suites for ZooKeeper and Consul. \newline\texttt{gemini-3-flash} produced the stronger Spring Framework suite. The comparison suggests that model performance is architecture-dependent rather than universally dominated by one model, which is consistent with prior results showing that LLM effectiveness changes with the explicitness and formality of the target architectural knowledge \cite{schindler2024,su2026evaluating}.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection{Threats to validity}\mbox{}\\\hspace*{\parindent}
Internal validity: The workflow depends on probabilistic LLM generation and review. A different run could produce a different package mapping, exception set, or convergence path. The use of \texttt{claude-opus-4-7} as the reviewer model may also bias the process toward artifacts generated by \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6}. Finally, the triage loop assumes that the reviewer can reliably distinguish mapping errors from real architectural drift; incorrect triage could suppress a valid constraint or preserve a false positive.

External validity: The evaluation covers three repositories and two languages: Spring Framework, Apache ZooKeeper, and HashiCorp Consul in Java and Go. The results may not generalize to other languages, architectural styles, enforcement tools, or projects without usable architecture documentation.

Construct validity: The evaluation treats the ArchAgent benchmark documentation as ground truth, but the documentation may be incomplete, high-level, or stale relative to the current implementation. In that case, a reported violation may reflect documentation drift rather than code drift. In addition, a 0-violation baseline does not guarantee architectural fidelity if the suite reaches that state through broad suppressions, synthetic layers, or overly permissive mappings.

Conclusion validity: The sample size is too small for statistical claims about model superiority. The results support qualitative conclusions about feasibility, failure modes, and the need for hybrid oversight, but larger studies across more repositories, pull requests, and languages are needed before making general claims about model ranking.


\subsection{Limitations}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Inputs
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Incomplete coverage: The ground truth documentation does not specify every package and dependency present in the current codebase.
        \item Ambiguity: Some constraints are described at a high level, so mapping to concrete packages requires judgment \cite{su2026evaluating}.
        \item Staleness: The documentation may lag behind the current repository version, so some violations may reflect documentation drift rather than code drift.
        \item Granularity mismatch: The documentation may define subsystems while enforcement operates on packages and import paths.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Outputs
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Over strict or over permissive rules: Generated constraints can block legitimate dependencies or miss real drift.
        \item Vacuous constraints risk: Rules can execute successfully while failing to meaningfully constrain the architecture.
        \item LLM hallucination: Generated artifacts can reference functions, classes, or configuration fields that do not exist.
        \item Mapping sensitivity: Incorrect package mappings can produce outputs that enforce the wrong boundaries.
        \item Fragility to refactors: Package restructuring can require rule updates even when architectural intent is unchanged.
        \item Execution scope limits: The checks only cover the code included in the configured build and scan scope.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Systems and datasets
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Scope of evaluated systems: The replication package contains completed artifacts for Spring Framework, Apache ZooKeeper, and HashiCorp Consul only.
        \item Documentation dependency: The approach assumes the availability of ArchAgent benchmark ground truth documentation for each target repository.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Language support
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Supported languages: Java and Go
        \item Not supported: Other languages and enforcement frameworks are out of scope in this implementation.
    \end{itemize}
    \item Evaluation limitations
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Cost and API usage: The workflow depends on paid IDE access and constrained API usage.
        \item Pull request availability: Drift detection depends on the availability and representativeness of open pull requests.
        \item Manual judgment: Some mapping decisions require manual review when documentation is ambiguous or outdated.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Examples of correct and incorrect cases}\mbox{}\\\hspace*{\parindent}
Correct case: HashiCorp Consul's Server/Consensus tier was a strong alignment case. The architecture documentation identifies Consul servers as a distinct Raft-backed control-plane component. \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} mapped this intent to the \texttt{agent/consul} package family and encoded additional intra-server constraints for state, FSM, and discovery-chain boundaries. This case aligned documentation, package structure, and generated constraints, resulting in a stable 0-violation baseline.

Incorrect or weak case: Spring Framework's cross-cutting utilities exposed a documentation-to-code mismatch. The documentation presents clean boundaries between Core Container, AOP, Data Access, Web, and other modules, but the implementation contains practical bridge utilities such as validation helpers, cache infrastructure, and exception-translation paths. When those utilities were forced into strict layers, \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-6} introduced synthetic layers and broad suppressions to reach a passing state. This shows that a passing suite can still be architecturally weak if the generated rules accommodate code reality by weakening the documented model too much.

\section{Conclusions and future work}
This project shows that LLMs can generate executable architectural rules, but reliable drift prevention requires a hybrid workflow that combines LLM generation, deterministic enforcement, and repository-aware engineer oversight \cite{schindler2024,su2026evaluating}. Across the evaluated repositories, both generation models eventually produced stable suites with 0 baseline violations, but the convergence paths required compile fixes, schema fixes, mapping corrections, and reviewer-guided triage.

The main lesson is that enforcement quality cannot be judged by compilation and 0 baseline violations alone. The fix histories, reviewer findings, and pull request results show that a suite can pass by using broad suppressions, synthetic layers, or incomplete mappings. The stronger result is the suite that both passes and preserves the documented architectural intent. Pull request drift detection is also suite-dependent: ZooKeeper PR-2307 failed under one generated suite but passed under the other. This complements architecture recovery research by showing that recovered or translated architecture must be operationalized, reviewed, and maintained to prevent future drift \cite{pan2026}.

\subsection{Future Work}
Future work should improve the generation workflow by requiring explicit mapping 
rationales, automated checks for broad suppressions, vacuous rules, and synthetic 
layers, and repository-aware engineer review before a suite is accepted. The 
generation step should also be extended by combining LLM translation with inductive 
rule learning \cite{schindler2024} and by treating collected fix histories as 
training data to produce more accurate translators over time. The evaluation should 
be broadened to additional languages and enforcement frameworks, more repositories, 
more pull requests, and controlled drift injections so that drift-detection behavior 
can be compared across known correct and incorrect cases. Finally, the generated 
rules should be studied inside a CI pipeline to evaluate whether they serve as 
practical and reliable merge-time safeguards.
